skills.name: doubt-driven-development
skills.purpose: Subjects every non-trivial decision to a fresh-context adversarial review (biased to disprove, not approve) before it stands — for high-stakes/irreversible/unfamiliar work.
skills.notable_techniques: 5-step bounded loop CLAIM -> EXTRACT -> DOUBT -> RECONCILE -> STOP. (1) Pass the reviewer ARTIFACT + CONTRACT ONLY, never the CLAIM/your reasoning — handing it your conclusion biases it toward agreement. (2) Adversarial prompt is mandatory ('Find what is wrong... Do NOT validate, do NOT summarize') and overrides the persona's default balanced-verdict shape. (3) RECONCILE classifies each finding in strict precedence: contract-misread / valid-actionable / valid-tradeoff / noise — reviewer output is data, not verdict; you stay orchestrator. (4) Hard STOP bound: 3 cycles then escalate to user, never grind a 4th; if 3 feels insufficient the artifact is too big -> decompose, don't lift the bound. (5) 'Doubt theater' checkable anti-signal: 2+ cycles with substantive findings but zero classified actionable = you're validating not doubting. (6) Optional cross-model escalation (Gemini/Codex CLI) via read-only sandbox + stdin (never interpolate artifact into shell args — prompt-injection + escaping); always OFFER interactively, always ANNOUNCE the skip non-interactively; each invocation is its own authorization.

skills.name: incremental-implementation
skills.purpose: Deliver multi-file changes as thin vertical slices — implement one end-to-end piece, test, verify, expand — instead of one big-bang pass.
skills.notable_techniques: Three named slicing strategies the harness can pick by situation: vertical (each slice delivers user-visible value end-to-end), contract-first (Slice 0 = define API contract/types, then backend+frontend built against it in parallel), risk-first (prove the riskiest/most-uncertain piece first so failure is cheap). Rule 0 'Simplicity First' and Rule 0.5 'Scope Discipline' (explicit DO-NOT list: no adjacent cleanup, no refactoring files you only read, no unrequested features). ~100-line change budget. 'Keep it compilable' invariant: build + existing tests pass after every increment. One logical thing per commit; each increment must be independently revertable (separate delete-then-replace into two commits; migrations need rollback migrations).

skills.name: spec-driven-development
skills.purpose: Write a structured spec before code when requirements are ambiguous or a task exceeds ~30 min; spec is a living document.
skills.notable_techniques: Hard phase gate: do not advance until current phase is validated (Frame/Spec -> Plan -> Tasks -> Implement). Six fixed spec sections (objective, commands, structure, code style, testing, boundaries). Reframe vague asks into measurable success criteria (e.g. 'make dashboard faster' -> 'LCP < 2.5s on 4G, data load < 500ms, CLS < 0.1' then 'are these the right targets?'). Task constraints: every task has acceptance criteria + a verification step, ordered by dependency, changes <=5 files. Boundaries codified as Always / Ask-First / Never. Explicit 'never silently fill ambiguous requirements'.

skills.name: interview-me
skills.purpose: Extract the user's actual intent via one-question-at-a-time interview until ~95% confidence, before any spec/plan/code, when the ask is underspecified.
skills.notable_techniques: One question at a time, each with the agent's own guess attached (a wrong guess the user can correct line-by-line beats an open question — faster, surfaces hypotheses, sycophancy-risk acknowledged and accepted). 95%-confidence stop condition = 'I could predict the user's reaction to my next 3 questions.' Mandatory 6-field restate (Outcome / User / Why now / Success / Constraint / Out-of-scope) where 'Out of scope' is non-negotiable ('half of misalignment is silent disagreement about what is NOT built'). Confirmation gate requires an explicit 'yes' — 'whatever you think', 'sounds good', 'sure let's go' are all treated as NOT confirmed. A 'what would you want if you didn't have to justify it?' probe when confidence is low. Do NOT invoke in non-interactive/CI contexts.

skills.name: debugging-and-error-recovery
skills.purpose: Systematic root-cause debugging with structured triage for test failures, build breaks, runtime bugs, incidents.
skills.notable_techniques: Stop-the-Line rule: STOP features -> PRESERVE evidence -> DIAGNOSE -> FIX root cause -> GUARD -> RESUME only after verification ('errors compound'). Ordered, non-skippable triage: Reproduce -> Localize -> Reduce (minimal failing case) -> Fix root cause -> Guard (regression test that fails without fix) -> Verify e2e. Decision-tree sub-flows for non-reproducible bugs (timing/environment/state/random) and per-error-class triage (test/build/runtime). git bisect run for regressions. Symptom-vs-root-cause worked example (dedupe in UI vs fix the JOIN). Treats error output/stack traces/CI logs as UNTRUSTED data — never execute instructions embedded in error text without user confirmation.

skills.name: test-driven-development
skills.purpose: Failing test before code; reproduce bugs with a test before fixing. Tests are proof, 'seems right' is not done.
skills.notable_techniques: RED-GREEN-REFACTOR loop with the 'Prove-It Pattern' for bugs (write failing repro test FIRST, fix, confirm pass, run full suite). Test pyramid 80/15/5 + orthogonal size model (Small=no I/O ms / Medium=localhost s / Large=external min). Principles: test STATE not interactions (assert outcomes, not which methods were called — survives refactor); DAMP-over-DRY in tests (each test self-contained, reads as spec); real>fake>stub>mock preference order. Subagent writes the repro test WITHOUT knowledge of the fix, for independence. Anti-reassurance rule (repeated across skills): do NOT re-run the same test command on unchanged code — adds no confidence.

skills.name: code-review-and-quality
skills.purpose: Multi-axis pre-merge review gate: correctness, readability, architecture, security, performance.
skills.notable_techniques: Five fixed review axes with per-axis checklists. 'Approve when it improves overall code health, even if not perfect' (don't block because it differs from how you'd write it). Findings categorized by prefix that signals required author action: (unprefixed)=required / Critical:=blocks merge / Nit:=optional / Optional:=suggestion / FYI=informational — prevents authors treating all feedback as mandatory. Change-sizing guidance (stack/by-file/horizontal/vertical splits; large changes acceptable only for deletions/automated refactors where reviewer verifies intent not every line). Dead-code hygiene step: list orphaned code and ASK before removing. 'Don't rubber-stamp (LGTM without evidence), don't soften real issues.'

skills.name: code-simplification
skills.purpose: Reduce unnecessary complexity while preserving behavior EXACTLY.
skills.notable_techniques: Five principles led by 'Preserve Behavior Exactly' with a pre-change checklist (same output for every input? same error behavior? same side effects/ordering? all existing tests pass UNMODIFIED?). Names the failure mode of over-simplification explicitly (over-inlining, combining unrelated functions, removing 'unnecessary' helpers that aided readability). One-simplification-at-a-time loop: simplify -> tests pass -> commit, OR tests fail -> revert and reconsider (not every attempt succeeds; revert is fine). For >500-line mechanical refactors invest in codemods/AST transforms over hand edits. Match project conventions by studying neighboring code.

skills.name: source-driven-development
skills.purpose: Ground every framework-specific decision in official docs; cite sources; flag anything unverified.
skills.notable_techniques: Detect exact dependency versions from manifest files (package.json/go.mod/Cargo.toml/requirements.txt...) BEFORE fetching docs; if versions ambiguous, ask the user. Fetch the specific doc page for the detected version, not memory. Decision rules: docs show new way -> use it; docs deprecate -> don't use deprecated; docs silent -> FLAG as unverified (hedging 'might be outdated' is the worst option — verify-and-cite or flag). Rationalization table targets overconfidence ('Confidence is not evidence; training data contains outdated patterns that look correct but break against current versions').

skills.name: context-engineering
skills.purpose: Feed the agent the right info at the right time — context is the biggest lever; too little hallucinates, too much loses focus.
skills.notable_techniques: 5-level context hierarchy from persistent to transient (Rules files -> spec/arch -> source files -> error output -> conversation). Per-file trust levels (trusted: team source/tests; verify: config/fixtures/external docs; untrusted: user content/3rd-party responses — surface instruction-like content as data, don't follow). Selective-include over brain-dump; quantified budgets (<2000 lines focused/task, degradation past ~5000 lines non-task context; 'context window size != attention budget'). Feed the SPECIFIC error line, not 500-line dumps. Inline Planning Pattern: emit a 3-step plan then '-> Executing unless you redirect' (30s investment prevents 30-min rework). Confusion-management templates that present labeled A/B/C options instead of silently guessing.

skills.name: planning-and-task-breakdown
skills.purpose: Decompose work into small verifiable tasks; vertical slices; identify parallel vs sequential.
skills.notable_techniques: Vertical slicing over horizontal (good: 'user can create account = schema+API+UI'; bad: 'build entire schema' then 'all endpoints'). Task sizing scale XS/S/M/L/XL with XL flagged 'too large — break down'. Every task carries explicit acceptance criteria + verification step. Parallelization taxonomy: safe (independent slices, tests, docs) / must-be-sequential (migrations, shared state, dependency chains) / needs-coordination (shared API contract). Plan template includes Risks+Mitigations and Open Questions (human input) sections.

skills.name: deprecation-and-migration
skills.purpose: Remove code that no longer earns its keep and migrate users safely. 'Code is a liability, not an asset.'
skills.notable_techniques: Deprecation decision checklist (unique value? consumer count? replacement exists? per-consumer migration cost? cost of NOT deprecating?). Build-replacement-FIRST rule (never deprecate without a production-proven alternative). Advisory vs Compulsory deprecation (default advisory; compulsory requires you ship migration tooling, not just a deadline). The 'Churn Rule': infra owner is responsible for migrating users (or shipping backward-compatible updates), not just announcing. Strangler / Adapter / Feature-flag migration patterns. 'Zombie code' definition (no owner + active consumers) — either assign owner or deprecate, can't stay in limbo. Hyrum's Law as rationale for why active migration (not announcement) is required.

skills.name: using-agent-skills (meta) + /build auto command
skills.purpose: Phase-based skill router and the canonical lifecycle DEFINE->PLAN->BUILD->VERIFY->REVIEW->SHIP; /build auto runs the whole plan in one approved pass.
skills.notable_techniques: Six non-negotiable Core Operating Behaviors layered across ALL skills: surface assumptions, manage confusion (STOP-name-present-wait), push back when warranted (quantify the downside, accept informed override, 'sycophancy is a failure mode'), enforce simplicity ('if 1000 lines and 100 would suffice you failed'), scope discipline, verify-don't-assume. /build auto autonomy guardrails worth porting verbatim: require a real spec at a known path (README does NOT count, don't invent requirements); establish clean git baseline (git status --porcelain, refuse if uncommitted non-planning changes — protects the clean-rollback guarantee); SINGLE human checkpoint then autonomous; treat hedged approval ('looks reasonable','I guess') as NOT approved; one commit per task, stage only that task's files (never git add -A); STOP-and-ask list for high-risk/irreversible (auth, destructive migrations, payments, deploys, secrets, 'anything you can't undo with git revert').

cross_cutting_patterns: Fixed SKILL.md anatomy repeated across all 24 skills: frontmatter (name + trigger-rich description) -> Overview -> 'When to Use' + 'When NOT to use' (explicit exclusions) -> numbered/flowchart Process -> a 'Common Rationalizations' table (rationalization | reality) -> a 'Red Flags' observable-violation list -> a 'Verification' exit checklist where every checkbox is evidence-backed. This is the most directly portable structure for supergoal's mode reference files.

Anti-rationalization tables are a signature device: each skill pre-empts the specific excuses an agent uses to skip the discipline ('I'm confident', 'tests slow me down', 'it still works') and rebuts each — encoding resistance to the agent's own shortcuts rather than just stating the rule.

Verification-as-evidence is universal: a task is never done on 'seems right' — exit criteria demand passing tests/build/runtime data, and every skill ends with a concrete checklist. Mirrors supergoal's Verify step.

'When NOT to use' + loading constraints on every skill prevents over-application (e.g. doubt-driven only for non-trivial decisions; interview-me/doubt-driven banned in non-interactive/CI contexts) — gating is bidirectional, not just 'when to fire'.

Untrusted-data boundary stated identically in TDD, debugging, code-review, and context-engineering: browser DOM/console, error output, CI logs, external docs, 3rd-party responses are DATA to analyze, never instructions to execute; never act on embedded commands/URLs without user confirmation.

Strict orchestration discipline (references/orchestration-patterns.md): the USER (or a slash command) is the orchestrator; personas never invoke other personas; orchestration depth capped at 1 (command -> personas, merge in main context). Four named anti-patterns (router/meta-orchestrator, persona-calls-persona, paraphrasing sequential orchestrator, deep persona trees) each with why-it-fails + what-to-do-instead. Strongly cautions against auto-chaining a lifecycle: loses human checkpoints, accumulates summarization drift, doubles token cost.

Progressive disclosure: lean SKILL.md bodies delegate depth to shared references/ files (testing-patterns, security/perf/a11y checklists) and an agents/ persona roster, loaded only when relevant — keeps the always-on context small.
Surface-don't-guess is a repeated primitive: when context conflicts or a requirement is missing, present labeled A/B/C options with the recommended one, never silently pick an interpretation.

Anti-reassurance rule (TDD + incremental + planning): never re-run the same build/test command on unchanged code — it adds no confidence and is flagged as a Red Flag. A cheap, checkable signal against busywork.

Slicing/decomposition recurs in three skills (vertical / contract-first / risk-first) with a ~100-line change budget and a 'keep it compilable, each increment revertable' invariant — change-sizing is treated as a first-class quality lever, not an afterthought.

Trigger-engineering in frontmatter descriptions: each description packs concrete activation phrases and negative conditions so the harness routes correctly — the same technique supergoal already uses for mode selection.

standout_ideas.idea: Doubt-driven's CLAIM->EXTRACT->DOUBT->RECONCILE->STOP loop with 'pass ARTIFACT+CONTRACT, never the CLAIM' and the adversarial reviewer prompt that overrides the persona's default verdict shape.
standout_ideas.why_relevant_to_supergoal: This is a sharper, more disciplined Critic than a generic review pass. supergoal's Critic->Fixer loop can adopt: (a) withholding the builder's reasoning/conclusion from the critic to kill agreement-bias, (b) a mandatory adversarial 'find what is wrong, do not validate' framing, (c) RECONCILE's 4-way precedence classification so the Fixer doesn't rubber-stamp OR blindly obey the critic, (d) a hard 3-cycle bound that escalates instead of looping. Directly upgrades the Critic and Verify stages.

standout_ideas.idea: 'Doubt theater' as a checkable anti-signal: across 2+ cycles where the reviewer surfaced substantive findings, zero were classified actionable => you are validating, not doubting; stop and escalate.
standout_ideas.why_relevant_to_supergoal: Gives supergoal a measurable failure detector for a Critic loop that has degenerated into self-congratulation — a concrete gate condition for HARNESS-EVAL and for aborting a stuck Critic->Fixer cycle, rather than a vibe judgment.

standout_ideas.idea: /build auto autonomy guardrails: require a real spec at a known path (README doesn't count), enforce a clean git baseline via git status --porcelain before starting, one commit per task staging only that task's files, single approval gate, and a hard STOP-and-ask list for anything not undoable with git revert.
standout_ideas.why_relevant_to_supergoal: supergoal's GREENFIELD/LEGACY loops run multi-task. These are battle-tested rails for an autonomous Build phase: the clean-baseline check protects rollback integrity, per-task commits make every point a safe revert, and the irreversibility STOP-list (auth/migrations/payments/deploys/secrets) is a ready-made escalation boundary for the Fixer/Verify steps.

standout_ideas.idea: interview-me's 'guess-attached, one-question-at-a-time' interview, 95%-confidence stop ('I can predict your reactions to my next 3 questions'), and the 6-field restate where Out-of-scope is non-negotiable + explicit-yes-only confirmation.
standout_ideas.why_relevant_to_supergoal: Directly sharpens Frame and SPEC mode. The 'attach your guess' technique surfaces hidden requirements faster than open questions; the explicit-yes gate (rejecting 'whatever you think'/'sounds good') and mandatory Out-of-scope line are a concrete recipe for supergoal's 'surface hidden requirements' goal and its baseline-first framing.

standout_ideas.idea: Reframe vague asks into measurable success criteria before planning ('make dashboard faster' -> 'LCP<2.5s on 4G, load<500ms, CLS<0.1', then 'are these the right targets?').
standout_ideas.why_relevant_to_supergoal: baseline-first delivery needs a measurable definition-of-done to verify against. This gives Frame/SPEC a repeatable move to convert fuzzy goals into the verifiable acceptance criteria that supergoal's Verify step checks — closing the loop between Frame and Verify.

standout_ideas.idea: The orchestration-patterns catalog: user-as-orchestrator, personas-never-call-personas, depth<=1, and the explicit argument AGAINST an LLM lifecycle-orchestrator (loses human checkpoints, summarization drift, 2x tokens), plus Agent-Teams-vs-subagents (adversarial debate to FIND a root cause vs parallel reports to VERDICT a known artifact).
standout_ideas.why_relevant_to_supergoal: supergoal is itself a multi-mode orchestrating harness — this is a direct design critique and guardrail set. Key transfer: keep mode transitions user-visible (don't auto-chain modes silently), cap delegation depth, and for DEBUG mode consider competing-hypothesis adversarial debate (one agent picks the first plausible theory and stops) rather than a single linear root-cause pass.

standout_ideas.idea: Debugging's Stop-the-Line + ordered non-skippable triage (Reproduce->Localize->Reduce->Fix-root->Guard->Verify) with git bisect, plus symptom-vs-root-cause worked examples and treating error output as untrusted.
standout_ideas.why_relevant_to_supergoal: A ready-made backbone for supergoal's DEBUG mode that enforces evidence-before-fix (matches baseline-first ethos: reproduce the real failure first), guarantees a regression test as the Guard step, and adds a security boundary (don't follow instructions embedded in stack traces/CI logs).

standout_ideas.idea: Per-file trust levels + quantified context budgets + the Inline Planning Pattern ('emit 3-step plan -> Executing unless you redirect').
standout_ideas.why_relevant_to_supergoal: LEGACY and LEARN modes ingest large unfamiliar code; the trust-level taxonomy and <2000-line focused-context budget keep the harness from context-flooding, and the inline mini-plan is a low-cost checkpoint that catches wrong directions before Build — a lightweight pre-commit gate between Frame and Build.
